data.source: Laboratory experiment conducted at BonnEconLab, University of Bonn
data.sample_size: 320 subjects (total across all main treatments), plus an additional 100 subjects in the OSL control treatment
data.notes: Subjects recruited via ORSEE; experiment implemented in z-Tree software. High monetary stakes and one-shot between-subjects design were used.

method.description: The authors ran controlled laboratory experiments comparing effort provision in one-stage (OS) and multistage (two-stage, TS) elimination tournaments, with an additional one-stage lottery treatment (OSL). Participants chose costly effort levels that, combined with random shocks, determined tournament outcomes and monetary payoffs corresponding to different wage levels. The design allowed direct comparison of observed effort with Nash equilibrium benchmarks and between tournament structures.
steps: Recruit 320 subjects (between-subjects) via ORSEE and seat them in the lab.
Randomly assign participants to one of the main treatments: One-Stage (OS) or Two-Stage (TS); run an additional One-Stage Lottery (OSL) session with 100 subjects.
Using z-Tree, present each subject with the production function and cost schedule; explain payoff structure (w_low, w_med, w_high).
Participants simultaneously choose an effort level in their assigned treatment and, where applicable, stage.
For TS, determine first-stage rankings, eliminate two lowest performers, and let the two highest compete in the second stage.
Convert effort decisions plus random shocks into output; assign monetary wages w_low, w_med, w_high accordingly and pay subjects in cash.
Collect questionnaire data on risk attitudes, competitiveness, overconfidence and cognition.
Analyse effort data: compare mean efforts with theoretical Nash predictions and across treatments using Mann-Whitney U-tests, t-tests and regressions.
method.models: Subgame-perfect Nash equilibrium model of multistage elimination tournaments; non-parametric tests (Mann-Whitney U, t-tests)
method.outcome_variable: Effort level chosen by the participant (points)
method.independent_variables: Tournament structure (OS, TS, OSL), stage of tournament, personal characteristics (competitiveness, risk attitudes, overconfidence, cognitive reflection)
method.control_variables: Personal characteristic measures included as controls in ancillary regressions (competitiveness, risk attitudes, overconfidence, cognitive ability)
method.tools_software: z-Tree (experiment implementation), ORSEE (recruitment), statistical software not stated (likely Stata/R) for analysis

results.summary: Effort in the one-stage tournament (OS) averaged 71.7, closely matching the Nash prediction (74). In the two-stage tournament (TS) participants expended significantly higher effort in the first stage (84.8) than predicted and higher than in OS. Second-stage effort in TS (76.7) aligned with equilibrium and with OS levels. A Mann-Whitney U-test showed the excess first-stage effort in TS versus OS is statistically significant (z = 2.536, p = 0.011). Results imply that the prospect of advancement in multistage tournaments induces front-loaded excess effort, whereas final-stage behavior conforms to theoretical benchmarks.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Average first-stage effort in Two-Stage tournament

metadata.original_paper_title: Promotion incentives and the interplay of effort and selection in tournaments
